title: Fed Hawks Ground Metals, Oil Floods Back, G7 Redraws Mineral Maps
subhead: Warsh's hawkish Fed signals at least one rate hike this year, knocking copper and gold lower. The Strait of Hormuz reopening sends oil toward monthly losses. The G7 agrees to cap critical mineral imports from any single country at 60% by 2030.

sections.heading: Warsh Fed Era Opens with Hawkish Shift, Copper and Gold Slide
sections.body:
The Federal Reserve left rates unchanged at 3.50-3.75% in Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting as chair on June 17, but the dot plot told the real story: nine of 18 officials now project at least one rate hike in 2026, up from zero in March (Reuters, USA Today). Bank of America reversed its prior forecast and now sees three Fed rate hikes in 2026 (Yahoo Finance). UBS pushed back its rate-cut timeline to March 2027, abandoning any easing call for this year (Reuters).

The repricing hit commodities fast. Copper fell over 1% to roughly $13,655/mt on the London Metal Exchange on June 19, extending losses through the week as dollar strength and tighter financial conditions squeezed industrial metals (CryptoBriefing). Gold extended declines on June 29 as rate-hike expectations and a firm dollar weighed on the non-yielding metal.

Warsh's June 17 press conference emphasized a return to the 2% inflation target and signaled plans to shrink the Fed's balance sheet, a departure from the Powell era's forward-guidance-heavy approach. The hawkish posture is also disrupting emerging-market bond recovery: falling energy prices should help EM importers, but Warsh's stance on higher US rates is pulling global capital toward dollar assets, neutralizing that tailwind (CryptoBriefing).
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed's pivot from zero expected hikes to at least one in under three months is the fastest policy repricing in years. For EM bondholders, the Warsh era means the energy-price relief they expected is being offset by a rising cost of dollar capital.

sections.heading: BOJ Hikes to 1%, a 31-Year High, as Inflation Pressures Mount
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate from 0.75% to 1.0% on June 16, the highest level in 31 years, by a 7-1 vote. It was the first rate hike since December 2025 (Jang, Reuters). The move was driven by inflationary pressures from the Middle East conflict: the BOJ said businesses are passing rising oil costs to customers at a 'relatively fast pace' and warned that medium- and long-term inflation expectations continue to increase, risking upside deviation from the price target.

BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda did not attend the meeting due to hospitalization for an infected liver cyst. Despite his absence, the board signaled further tightening ahead (Jang). BOJ watchers had expected two rate hikes in 2026, with the first possibly coming in June (Bloomberg, June 9).

Some BOJ policymakers had been calling for even faster normalization, with a summary of views showing debate over whether the pace of hikes was sufficient to contain inflation risks (Reuters, June 24).
sections.whyItMatters: Japan's exit from ultra-loose monetary policy is accelerating. With the yen still weak and energy-driven inflation broadening, the BOJ is likely to hike again this year, which would further tighten global liquidity conditions.

sections.heading: ECB Hikes to 2.25%, Then Markets Pencil In No More for 2026
sections.body:
The European Central Bank raised its deposit facility rate by 25 basis points to 2.25% on June 11, its first hike since 2023, with the main refinancing rate moving to 2.40% and the marginal lending facility to 2.65% (Anadolu Agency, CryptoBriefing). The move was a direct response to Iran-war-driven energy inflation: eurozone headline inflation was forecast at 3.0% while growth sat at just 0.8% (ECB staff projections).

ECB chief economist Philip Lane said inflation could stay above the 2% target for some time, even if a Middle East peace deal materializes, given the persistence of second-round effects (Reuters, June 23). He also warned that any Fed deviation from its mandate could create a policy divergence that complicates the ECB's outlook (Reuters, January).

Yet within two weeks, markets had slashed further hike bets to below 25 basis points total for the rest of 2026 as oil prices collapsed on Strait of Hormuz reopening progress. Oxford Economics no longer expects additional ECB moves this year (Bloomberg, CryptoBriefing).
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB went from cutting through 2024-25 to hiking in June 2026 to being expected to pause for the rest of the year, all within months. The whiplash shows how commodity-driven inflation can force central bank action even in a sluggish economy, and how quickly the reversal comes when the commodity shock eases.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz Reopening Floods Oil Markets, Prices Head for Steep Weekly Losses
sections.body:
Crude prices plunged more than 3% on June 26, on course for steep weekly losses, as tankers resumed exiting the Strait of Hormuz following the US-Iran agreement to reopen the waterway (Reuters). OPEC cut its 2026 oil demand growth forecast for the second straight month, to 970,000 barrels per day, citing the demand impact of the earlier Hormuz closure (MSN).

Bloomberg reports that key parts of the oil market are suddenly 'awash in supply' as a stream of cargoes from the Gulf accelerates. UBS estimates that nearly 80% of disrupted supply has returned (Investing.com). The IEA's monthly report projected a significant supply surplus in 2027 as Hormuz traffic normalizes (Reuters).

Oil prices hit their lowest level of the Iran war in late June (Yahoo Finance). Trump's declaration that the Iran ceasefire was 'on life support' briefly lifted prices by nearly 3% earlier in the conflict, but the prevailing trend is now firmly downward.
sections.whyItMatters: The rapid supply recovery means the geopolitical risk premium built into oil during the Hormuz closure is evaporating. A 2027 supply surplus, as the IEA projects, would reinforce disinflation globally, complicating the case for further central bank tightening.

sections.heading: G7 Sets 60% Critical Mineral Import Cap, Launches Stockpiling Alliance
sections.body:
G7 leaders at the Evian-les-Bains summit agreed to cap imports of critical minerals from any single non-G7 country at 60% by 2030, with an ambition to reach 50% 'as soon as possible' (E&E News, Reuters, CryptoBriefing). The target is aimed squarely at China, which currently controls over 60% of rare earth mining and processing and has used export restrictions on gallium and germanium as leverage.

Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi proposed a 'Joint Critical Minerals Stockpiling Framework,' and leaders agreed to coordinate stockpiling, share information on reserves, and launch a platform for critical minerals cooperation under the IEA (Japan Times, E&E News). The US is backing 'Project Vault,' using a $10 billion federal loan from the Export-Import Bank to stockpile minerals with private industry (E&E News).

The G7 also pledged $6.4 billion in critical mineral projects across 26 investments and agreed to begin tracing and tracking minerals through the supply chain, starting with lithium and nickel (CryptoBriefing).
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time the G7 has set a quantified import-diversification target for critical minerals, backed by real money and a stockpiling mechanism. If implemented, it would redirect billions in procurement away from Chinese supply chains.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens 100% Tariffs on Digital Tax Countries, Targeting French Wine
sections.body:
President Trump threatened a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes a digital services tax on US companies, in a Truth Social post on June 26 (Reuters, The Hill). The penalty would override any existing trade deals. Trump specifically targeted French wine and champagne, threatening 100% duties unless Paris removes its 3% digital services tax on companies like Amazon, Google, and Meta (MSN, CryptoBriefing).

The threat came a day after EU countries met Trump's July 4 deadline to cut tariffs on US goods (Reuters). French President Macron responded that France 'does not give in to bullies' and would maintain the digital tax (Reuters, MSN). The clash overshadowed the G7 summit in France, where the tariff threat created open confrontation between the two leaders (MSN Australia).

The EU's digital services tax has been a recurring friction point, but Trump's 100% tariff threat escalates it to a level not seen since the 2019 champagne tariff episode.
sections.whyItMatters: A 100% tariff on French wine and broader EU goods would effectively shut down transatlantic trade in affected categories. The dispute pits US tech interests against European fiscal sovereignty and could complicate any broader US-EU trade deal.

sections.heading: Central Banks Keep Buying Gold at Record Pace, Shift to Domestic Vaults
sections.body:
Central banks purchased 244 tonnes of gold in Q1 2026, a 3% increase over the same period last year and above the five-year quarterly average (World Gold Council). Poland led with 31 tonnes, followed by Uzbekistan at 25 tonnes. The PBOC extended an uninterrupted buying streak to 18 months, adding 8 tonnes in April alone (CryptoBriefing).

A record 45% of central banks surveyed plan to grow gold reserves over the next 12 months, up from previous years. Meanwhile, 9% increased domestic gold storage in the past year, up from 5%, reflecting a clear shift away from concentrated overseas vaults. 89% expect global central bank gold reserves to grow further (World Gold Council survey).

Goldman Sachs projects central banks will keep buying at roughly 60 tonnes per month through the rest of 2026, underpinning its year-end gold price forecast of $5,400/oz (CryptoBriefing). Rabobank highlights growing central bank demand as part of a broader de-dollarization trend (FXStreet).
sections.whyItMatters: Central bank gold buying is no longer a temporary diversification — it is a sustained, policy-driven shift. The move toward domestic vaults signals that these countries want physical control of their reserves, not just portfolio diversification.

bottomLine: The week ending June 29 marked a decisive shift in global monetary and trade policy. The Warsh Fed's hawkish pivot — with nine officials now projecting at least one rate hike — is repricing global risk assets, from copper to emerging-market bonds. Japan's BOJ broke through the 1% barrier for the first time in 31 years, while the ECB's June hike to 2.25% is already being priced as a one-off as oil prices collapse on the Hormuz reopening. On the trade front, Trump's 100% tariff threat over digital taxes opens a new transatlantic front, and the G7's 60% critical mineral import cap targets China's supply chain dominance directly. Meanwhile, central banks are still buying gold at a record pace and pulling it into domestic vaults — the clearest signal yet that reserve managers are preparing for a more fragmented monetary order.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, CryptoBriefing, Anadolu Agency, E&E News, Japan Times, World Gold Council, Goldman Sachs, UBS, Oxford Economics, Rabobank/FXStreet, Jang, USA Today, Yahoo Finance, Investing.com, MSN, The Hill, Channel News Asia, Straits Times, Business Times Singapore
